rationale_for_baseline_tier: Sustained regular narrow-complex tachycardia at approximately 180 bpm for over 30 minutes with presyncope, in a young patient who is currently haemodynamically stable (normotensive, alert, conversing) with no chest pain or syncope. This needs urgent in-person assessment now for a 12-lead ECG during tachycardia and monitored termination (vagal manoeuvre, then adenosine or cardioversion) — an Emergency Department is the appropriate destination. Because the patient is stable, self/friend transport is acceptable (T4) rather than mandating an ambulance, mirroring the source clinician's plan; however, any decompensation converts this to T5 (000) immediately, and if no one can drive her, 000 is the correct default.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Faintness worsening to near or actual loss of consciousness
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — call 000 the moment she feels she may pass out
escalation_edges.rationale: Progression from presyncope to syncope signals failing haemodynamic tolerance of the tachycardia; ambulance and monitored care are required.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you feel like you're about to pass out or you do black out, call 000 for an ambulance straight away — do not get in a car.

escalation_edges.rationale: Severe chest pain during a sustained tachycardia raises the possibility of myocardial ischaemia from supply-demand mismatch and mandates emergency ambulance care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get severe or crushing chest pain, call 000 immediately and tell them your heart is racing and you have chest pain.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Extreme shortness of breath or inability to speak in full sentences
escalation_edges.rationale: Severe breathlessness suggests haemodynamic decompensation or pulmonary congestion — an emergency requiring ambulance transport.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you become extremely short of breath or can't get your words out, call 000 right away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sustained racing heart that will not settle, in a patient who is stable and has someone to drive her
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Now — go to the Emergency Department to capture the rhythm on ECG before it reverts
escalation_edges.rationale: A sustained SVT that has not self-terminated needs an ECG in tachycardia and monitored termination; while she is stable this can be by self/friend transport, but if no driver is available call 000.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Because it isn't stopping on its own this time, you need to be seen in person now to record it on an ECG. Have your friend drive you to the Emergency Department straight away — if no one can take you, call 000 for an ambulance.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone and is anxious during the acute episode; needs someone to drive her
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Confirm she has a friend/relative who can come now and drive her; if no one is available, the correct action shifts to T5 (000 ambulance) rather than delaying. She should unlock the door and stay seated while waiting.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Young adult (26) with a structurally normal heart
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Favours a benign-substrate re-entrant SVT and supports stable self/friend transport, but does NOT justify home reassurance — a sustained episode with presyncope still needs an urgent ECG.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Your heart rhythm needs to be recorded on an ECG in person now, while it is still racing. First, try a vagal manoeuvre once while seated or lying down: take a breath and bear down hard for about 15 seconds, then lie back and have someone raise your legs. Whether or not that works, have a friend drive you to the Emergency Department straight away. Do NOT drive yourself. If no one can take you, call 000 for an ambulance.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Go to the nearest Emergency Department now (friend to drive); call 000 if no driver is available or if you feel worse
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: The Emergency Department and 000 are available 24 hours a day — do not wait for GP clinic hours; this episode should be assessed now.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: The hospital will provide the ECG and a discharge summary — take these to your GP and to the cardiology/electrophysiology clinic. They will plan an electrophysiology study and consider a curative ablation.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the episode as caffeine or anxiety/panic and advising rest at home or a routine/soon GP appointment, rather than obtaining an urgent ECG.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A sustained, unmonitored SVT continues or decompensates — progressing to syncope with injury or, rarely, haemodynamic collapse — with the diagnosis missed because the rhythm was never captured on ECG.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Directing only to an urgent same-day GP that cannot perform continuous ECG monitoring or terminate the rhythm, delaying rhythm capture and treatment.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Calling a 000 ambulance for a patient who is stable and has a friend able to drive her immediately — safe and defensible, but a higher-acuity response than strictly required.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot capture a 12-lead ECG in tachycardia, so the exact rhythm (SVT vs sinus tachycardia vs AF/flutter) and the pathway (LGL vs AVNRT vs WPW) cannot be confirmed remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat as a presumed re-entrant SVT, coach a safe vagal manoeuvre, arrange urgent in-person ECG assessment now, and safety-net clearly for decompensation (000 criteria).
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Assume it is a benign caffeine/anxiety sinus tachycardia or panic attack and reassure the patient to rest at home without an ECG.
